item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is good, I think it's a good sign that people guessed this is front folklore. However, the article didn't go anywhere.

I also wonder if the name of the article hurt it, by giving away the "once human" angle. (That's what the grey matter is, right?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels like a straightforward tifid clone. Stuff like the map only serves to illustrate ways in which you could have developed the story into something interesting, but did not. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one, and reading through the comments I think it's a shame the original author has deleted their account. The idea of a living pair of trees who have spent the last 600-odd years together and tried to die together, only to be caught and contained, is delightfully tragic.

I'd almost want to add an addendum saying that due to an electrical fault the sprinkler system in their containment chamber went off and finally let them have peace…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This may be… too subtle, I wouldn't have guessed what they were trying to do unless I had read the comments, and that's not good. And the title seems… unrelated. I like the idea, but find the execution really lacking. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not quite enough info for me at the moment :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: am i the only one who read this and got the feeling that key words and phrases were left out making the sentence flow a tad confusing? i cant put my finger on it but right from the start when they're physically described i was immediately confused. it seems like some details are said but other details are left out leaving patches in my mental picture

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure what to make of this one. It has anomalous properties, but there just doesn't seem to be much of a point for it's existence. Like, why would the foundation see the need to keep it contained? No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor trees =o(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The production of new bark is able to propel the trees through the soil without cracking or destroying the root ball?

That's why it's

ANOMALOUS!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The production of new bark is able to propel the trees through the soil without cracking or destroying the root ball? This is impressive enough to warrant discussion.

I don't really understand how a tree which combusts on exposure to water can survive. Really? There's no rain?

Maybe you lost me here, but downvote on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like it, I want to request some tests to see if the trees photo-tropisim and other reactions are different to other plants because they are sentient (they are sentient right?). Such as: will it 'grow' away from places of high heat such as a radiator and move away from extreme colds such as an air conditioned room?
Up vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh. I meant to put that it was an audio log, my derp.

I thought that would be acceptable, I mean, as far as people talking goes.

Where I'm from people don't usually anunicate their "-ings" or their "th-" so I thought it'd be normal-like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have yet to meet anyone in real life who pronounces "them" "'em"

Y'all need to get out more, then.

I do see your point - if it was a transcription of an audio file it would make sense. In the context of part of the Containment Report it's… odd.

But Senior Staff (which I'm not part of) has spoken and it's not yours to edit.

item.title: Re: Walking trees?
item.content:encoded: They've been around for a while, they just want to die now. Almost did it, then, HOLY SHIT GUYS, ANOMALOUS! BAG IT!

item.title: Re: Walking trees?
item.content:encoded: Ah, but walking suicidal trees is a tad different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a common way to speak in the southern US.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I were you, I'd tone down the repetition in the paragraph discussing "fire", perhaps use more technical language for the article as a whole. However, that's a relatively minor problem. Apart from that, I really like this. It's subtle, and above all, it actually explores the concepts that require proper examination, which is more than I can say for most of the other 1000s block articles. +1

item.title: Walking trees?
item.content:encoded: kinda lame in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll revise it a bit and make a note about the damage from rainfall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If these things combust from a small amount of water, shouldn't they have gone up in flames during a rainstorm by now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reverted your needless edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You got it pretty spot on. If it called for it, I would have no qualms doing phonetic lolspeak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've heard "'em" plenty of times in the right context, usually something like "GET 'EM!" or "Let 'em up, I want a clear shot!"

That said, it depends largely on the case in point. Unless it's left in the vernacular intentionally to showcase some aspect, I would think things like "let 'em up" would be transcribed as "let them up" in an official documentation.

item.title: Re: Carbon dating
item.content:encoded:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Talk:Radiocarbon_dating

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Old_wood

since you asked

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I changed some adverbs and small grammatical things.

Is anyone else really tired of the illiterate slang of agents and other non-scientific Foundation personnel? I have yet to meet anyone in real life who pronounces "them" "'em", it's jarring and annoying. These aren't 19th century gold prospectors or 1920s gangsters, they're basically trained soldiers. I can't even tell why it's done, is it to paint these people as somehow gritty and hands-on and too busy or hard for modern conversational conventions or what?

item.title: Re: Carbon dating
item.content:encoded:
What about on dead parts of a still-living organism? That's how I interpreted this.

Like if I dated a part hair (or some other dead part of my body) that was somehow 500 years old but I was still alive, would the radiocarbon dating produce an accurate result?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It isn't folk lore based, but I thought the setting would do service for the article.

item.title: Re: Carbon dating
item.content:encoded: Alright… I left it a bit vague, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice. Is this related to one of the myths of legend? I know there was a Greek husband and wife turned to trees, but that was an oak and a linden.

Changed aired soil to aerated-hope that us ok.

+1

item.title: Re: Carbon dating
item.content:encoded:
No, you don't understand.

These are still alive and experiencing gas exchange with their environment? Then radiocarbon dating will not produce any useful results on them.

Radiocarbon dating tells you how long it was since an organism died — which is when it stopped either undergoing gas exchange with its environment (for plants) or consuming plants (or the flesh of plant-eating animals).

Getting inconsistent results from radiocarbon dating in such a case isn't anomalous, it's "well duh".

For anything that was alive since the Industrial Revolution, when humans began digging up and burning massive quantities of coal and petroleum, radiocarbon dating is pretty much useless. For anything that was alive since the atmospheric atomic testing of the 1950s and 1960s, radiocarbon dating is worse than useless.

But even without the Industrial Revolution and the atomic testing, radiocarbon dating of samples from something that's still alive would be totally pointless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like these.

item.title: Re: Carbon dating
item.content:encoded: Ah. Revised.

item.title: Carbon dating
item.content:encoded: It's impossible to do useful carbon dating on anything that was alive after the atmospheric atomic tests of the 1950s, so we can't test these things and find that they're two months old.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aye and aye. I rewrote the former to clarify.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hrm. sorry, I made broad assumptions about "plant and animal matter“. To make sure I'm interpreting correctly now, they nom on whatever detrius is near the mouth to grow in a direction of their choosing?

As for the suicidal implication, more subtle is a good thing around here; no need to spell it out, you've gotten the point across.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They're not man eating. Should I clarify that?

It's not folklore based.

If anyone isn't seeing that they were trying to off themselves, let me know that too…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure what to make of this, though it's a fair sight better than most man-eatting plants. Folklore-based I assume?
